senses.definition: Small waves or gentle movements on the surface of water or another surface.
examples: The wind made tiny ripplings on the surface of the lake.
She watched the ripplings of the stream as it flowed over the stones.

senses.definition: Having many small waves or gentle curves on the surface.
examples: The ripply water reflected the setting sun.
She drew a ripply line to show the path of the river.

senses.definition: A family name of English origin, based on a place name related to the town of Ripon in North Yorkshire.
examples: Adam Rippon is a well‑known American figure skater.
The researcher Dr. Rippon gave a lecture on brain development.

senses.definition: Plural of the surname Ripp or Rippes, used as a family name.
examples: The Ripps were among the first families to settle in the village.
Several members of the Ripps attended the reunion.

senses.definition: Large stones or broken pieces of concrete placed on a shore, riverbank, or slope to prevent erosion.
examples: Engineers placed riprap along the riverbank to protect the road from flooding.
The coastline was lined with riprap to stop the waves from washing the land away.

senses.definition: Protected with riprap to stop soil or a bank from being worn away by water.
examples: The riverbank was riprapped last year to control erosion.
They riprapped the base of the bridge to keep the supports stable.

senses.definition: The act or process of covering a bank, shore, or slope with riprap.
examples: Riprapping of the canal sides began in early spring.
The project includes riprapping vulnerable sections of the coastline.

senses.definition: Covers or protects a bank, shore, or slope by placing riprap on it.
examples: The team ripraps the river edges before the rainy season begins.
The company regularly ripraps sections of the dam to keep it secure.

senses.definition: Tears something quickly and with force.
examples: He rips the envelope open without reading the name.
Be careful, or the tape will rip the paper when you pull it off.

senses.definition: Long narrow tears in cloth, paper, or other material.
examples: There are several small rips in my jeans.
The book had rips and stains on many pages.

senses.definition: A gray whale, especially the California gray whale.
examples: Early whalers sometimes called the gray whale a ripsack.
Ripsacks migrate thousands of kilometers along the Pacific coast each year.

senses.definition: A saw with teeth designed to cut wood along the grain.
examples: He used a ripsaw to cut the board into long strips.
The carpenter keeps a ripsaw and a crosscut saw on the same rack.

senses.definition: Cut with a ripsaw along the grain of the wood.
examples: They ripsawed the log into narrow boards for flooring.
The planks were ripsawed yesterday at the mill.

senses.definition: The action or process of cutting wood lengthwise using a ripsaw.
examples: Ripsawing is noisy, so workers wear ear protection.
Continuous ripsawing of the timber stack took most of the day.

senses.definition: Cut along the grain of the wood with a ripsaw.
examples: The table is made from ripsawn oak boards.
Ripsawn lumber is often used for structural framing.

senses.definition: More than one ripsaw.
examples: The workshop keeps several ripsaws for different types of wood.
Old ripsaws hung on the wall of the shed.

senses.definition: Something or someone that is extremely strong, exciting, or impressive.
examples: The storm last night was a real ripsnorter.
She told a ripsnorter of a story that had everyone laughing.

senses.definition: Plural of ripsnorter: things or people that are extremely strong, exciting, or impressive.
examples: The match had its share of ripsnorters on both sides.
He has written a few ripsnorters that became bestsellers.

senses.definition: Very exciting, energetic, or impressive.
examples: They had a ripsnorting good time at the festival.
The film is a ripsnorting adventure from start to finish.

senses.definition: In a way that is extremely exciting, energetic, or impressive.
examples: The show was ripsnortingly funny from beginning to end.
He played ripsnortingly well in the final match.

senses.definition: A stone or large rock that has been dropped into finer sediment and later found inside it, similar to a dropstone.
examples: The geologist pointed out a ripstone embedded in the mudstone layer.
Ripstones can show where icebergs once carried rocks across the sea.

senses.definition: A strong woven fabric, often nylon, made so that it does not tear easily.
examples: The tent is made from lightweight ripstop.
Ripstop is commonly used for outdoor clothing and gear.

senses.definition: Describing fabric that is woven in a way that stops small tears from spreading.
examples: He wore a jacket made of ripstop nylon.
The parachute is constructed from ripstop material to improve safety.

senses.definition: Pieces or types of ripstop fabric.
examples: Different ripstops are tested for strength and weight.
They compared several nylon ripstops before choosing one for the product line.

senses.definition: A nonstandard or dialect spelling of 'ripped', meaning tore something or became very muscular.
examples: He ript his shirt on the fence in one quick move.
The old letter was so fragile it almost ript in my hands.

senses.definition: A strong and often dangerous current in the sea where different flows of water meet.
examples: Swimmers are warned to stay away from the riptide near the rocks.
A sudden riptide pulled the boat away from the shore.

senses.definition: A strong emotional force or trend that is difficult to resist.
examples: News of the attack sent a riptide of fear through the city.
She felt a riptide of anger rising inside her.

senses.definition: More than one riptide, meaning several strong sea currents or strong forces that are hard to resist.
examples: Dangerous riptides form along this stretch of beach every summer.
The country was caught in political and social riptides.

senses.definition: Relating to the Ripuarian group of Central Franconian dialects spoken in parts of Germany, Belgium, and the Netherlands.
examples: He is doing research on Ripuarian dialects spoken near Cologne.
Ripuarian speech varies greatly from village to village.

senses.definition: A person who speaks a Ripuarian dialect.
examples: Many Ripuarians identify more with their local dialect than with standard German.
The survey included both Ripuarians and speakers of other Franconian varieties.

senses.definition: A nonstandard written form of 'rip up', meaning to tear something into small pieces.
examples: They decided to ripup the old contract and start again.
Angry, she ripup the letter and threw it in the bin.

senses.definition: The Māori name for the grey warbler, a small insect‑eating bird found in New Zealand.
examples: We heard the clear song of the riroriro in the bush.
The riroriro is one of the most common native birds in New Zealand forests.

senses.definition: Plural of riroriro, meaning more than one grey warbler.
examples: Several riroriros were flitting through the trees at dawn.
We saw riroriros and other native birds on the walk.

senses.definition: An Arabic term meaning a treatise or written work, especially one that explains religious law.
examples: The scholar wrote a short risala on prayer and fasting.
Many classic risalas on Islamic law are still studied today.

senses.definition: A mid‑level cavalry officer in the Indian and Pakistani armies, historically a commander of a cavalry unit in the British Indian Army.
examples: He served as a risaldar in an armoured regiment during the war.
The rank of risaldar dates back to the days of the British Indian Army.

senses.definition: Plural of risaldar, meaning several mid‑level cavalry officers.
examples: Risaldars played an important role in leading mounted units.
The museum display shows uniforms worn by former risaldars.

senses.definition: A narrow strip or ridge of material in a mine or quarry used to catch falling rocks or to separate working areas.
examples: The safety plan required a risberm along the edge of the haul road.
Loose stones collected on the risberm after the blasting.

senses.definition: An old European silver coin similar to a riksdaler, once used as a unit of money in some countries.
examples: The museum displays a 17th‑century risdaler among other coins.
He inherited a small collection of risdalers from his grandfather.

senses.definition: To move from a lower position to a higher one.
examples: The sun will rise at six tomorrow morning.
Smoke rose slowly from the chimney.

senses.definition: To increase in amount, level, or number.
examples: Prices have risen sharply this year.
The temperature is expected to rise in the afternoon.

senses.definition: An increase in size, amount, or level.
examples: Many workers are asking for a rise in pay.
There has been a rise in crime in this area.

senses.definition: Past participle of 'rise': having moved up or increased.
examples: The river has risen after the heavy rain.
Unemployment has risen in recent months.

senses.definition: A person who gets out of bed at a particular time.
examples: He is an early riser and starts work before seven.
Late risers often skip breakfast.

senses.definition: The vertical part at the front of a step in a staircase.
examples: Each riser must be the same height for safety.
The carpenter painted the risers white and left the treads natural.

senses.definition: Plural of riser, meaning people who get up at a certain time, or the vertical parts of steps.
examples: Early risers enjoy the quiet of the morning.
The steps had very high risers and narrow treads.

senses.definition: An Italian term on wine labels showing that the wine has been aged longer and is usually of higher quality than the basic version.
examples: We ordered a Chianti Riserva with dinner.
Only the best vintages are bottled as Riserva at this winery.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of 'rise': moves up or increases.
examples: The balloon rises slowly into the air.
Crime usually rises when unemployment is high.

senses.definition: Plural of rise, meaning several increases in amount or level.
examples: There have been several rises in interest rates this year.
Sharp rises in rent are worrying many families.

senses.definition: In Indian religions, a wise and enlightened sage, especially one who composed parts of the Vedas.
examples: The hymns are said to have been revealed to ancient rishis.
He read stories about a rishi meditating in the forest.

senses.definition: Plural of rishi, meaning several wise and enlightened sages in Indian traditions.
examples: Many rishis are remembered in Hindu texts for their teachings.
The Vedas are traditionally linked to seven great rishis.

senses.definition: A relative or kinsman, especially in South Asian usage influenced by Hindi and Urdu.
examples: He invited all his rishtadars to the wedding.
Several close rishtadars live in the same neighborhood.

senses.definition: A person’s ability or tendency to laugh, often treated in a humorous way.
examples: The comedian’s jokes tested the audience’s risibilities.
Her dry comments tickled our risibilities all evening.

senses.definition: The ability or tendency to laugh.
examples: His natural risibility made him the life of the party.
The film aims to appeal to our basic risibility.

senses.definition: Laughter or the act of laughing.
examples: A ripple of risibility ran through the audience.
His comment was met with quiet risibility rather than loud applause.

senses.definition: So silly or stupid that it deserves to be laughed at.
examples: The idea that the earth is flat is simply risible.
He made a risible attempt to explain away his mistake.

senses.definition: Relating to laughing or having a tendency to laugh.
examples: The muscles around the mouth are sometimes called the risible muscles.
Children are often in a risible mood during the holidays.

senses.definition: The quality of being laughable or ridiculous.
examples: Critics pointed out the risibleness of the proposal.
He seemed unaware of the risibleness of his own excuses.

senses.definition: A humorous term for a person’s ability to laugh or their sense of humor.
examples: The cartoon tickled my risibles.
Such jokes are meant to stir our risibles, not to offend us.

senses.definition: In a way that is so silly or inadequate that it deserves laughter.
examples: The workers were risibly underpaid for the amount of risk they took.
The report was so short it was almost risibly incomplete.

senses.definition: Going up in level, amount, or importance.
examples: There is rising concern about climate change.
She is a rising star in the tech industry.

senses.definition: An act of people fighting against a government or authority.
examples: The rising was quickly put down by the army.
Historians still debate the causes of the 1848 rising.

senses.definition: The process of coming up, especially of the sun, moon, or tide.
examples: We watched the rising of the sun over the hills.
The rising of the tide soon covered the rocks.
